paragraphs: Most CVs are a list of what you did. This is six positions on how I do it — the principles that show up in the code I write, the teams I run, and the bugs I cause. They're commitments, not observations: closer to what I'm trying to live up to than what I always pull off.

pledges.title: Culture is king
pledges.text: Inclusive teams produce better work than uniform ones. Cognitive diversity surfaces ideas a monoculture never finds, but only if people feel safe enough to argue — and the leader's job is to build the room people are willing to disagree in, then mostly get out of the way.

pledges.title: Framing is first
pledges.text: Most engineering trouble is a well-executed solution to a misframed problem. POCs are cheap and code is fast; what's expensive is six months of architecture optimised for the wrong question. I push hard on definitions before estimates, and I'm willing to spend a week not-knowing if it saves a quarter on the wrong build.

pledges.title: Documentation and clear decisions
pledges.text: If a decision is worth making, it's worth being able to argue with later. I keep an ADR log on every project of meaningful size — this site has thirty-one so far, including the ones that document deviations and reversals. Retros are the same habit at session-scale: I write down what surprised me, and the patterns that recur become tools.

pledges.title: Right-aligned development
pledges.text: I scan the board right-to-left before picking up new work — who needs a review, who's stuck on something I have context on, who's working in the same area I'm about to touch. Unblocking someone else is usually cheaper than unblocking yourself, and the team ships more when the right-hand side of the board moves first. Right-Aligned Development; there's a blog post in this somewhere.

pledges.title: Honesty over polish
pledges.text: I'd rather a CV bullet, an ADR, or a status update read truthfully than impressively. The JD-matcher on this site is deliberately tuned to surface my own gaps as "honest gaps" rather than invent matches, and the ADRs include one that admits I darkened a cream tone because the design was below WCAG AA. Polished claims that don't survive a follow-up question are worse than honest claims that do.

pledges.title: No absolutism in technological choices
pledges.text: Every technology choice is a tradeoff. I default to SOLID, maintainability, and agentic readiness (tools and structures an LLM agent can reason about) as the criteria — and I'll break any of them deliberately when the product needs a different balance, provided the deviation is written down. ADR-0001 picks Next.js over the design's recommended Astro; ADR-0007 darkens a cream against the design system to clear WCAG AA.
